Phase 3 — Base Population PK Model

One-compartment oral, first-order absorption (nlmixr2 / rxode2)

Author

Pramod BR

Published

June 28, 2026

1 Objectives

Having characterised the data (Phase 1) and computed model-free exposure metrics (Phase 2), we now fit a structural population PK model: a one-compartment model with first-order oral absorption. This phase establishes the model skeleton and the typical (population-average) parameters; between-subject variability and covariates are layered on in Phases 4–5.

2 From tidy data to NONMEM events

Estimation engines treat every row as an event — either a dose or an observation. build_nonmem_data() inserts one dosing row per subject at time 0 and keeps the concentration rows as observations.

Code 
dat <- build_nonmem_data()
dat |>
  dplyr::filter(ID == 1) |>
  kbl(caption = "Subject 1 as an event dataset (dose row first, then observations)",
      digits = 2) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
Subject 1 as an event dataset (dose row first, then observations)
ID TIME DV AMT EVID CMT MDV WT
1 0.00 NA 319.99 1 1 1 79.6
1 0.00 0.74 0.00 0 2 0 79.6
1 0.25 2.84 0.00 0 2 0 79.6
1 0.57 6.57 0.00 0 2 0 79.6
1 1.12 10.50 0.00 0 2 0 79.6
1 2.02 9.66 0.00 0 2 0 79.6
1 3.82 8.58 0.00 0 2 0 79.6
1 5.10 8.36 0.00 0 2 0 79.6
1 7.03 7.47 0.00 0 2 0 79.6
1 9.05 6.89 0.00 0 2 0 79.6
1 12.12 5.94 0.00 0 2 0 79.6
1 24.37 3.28 0.00 0 2 0 79.6

Column meanings: EVID = 1 marks the dose (with AMT in mg) and 0 marks an observation (with DV, the concentration); MDV = 1 means “no measured DV on this row” (true for dose rows); CMT routes the dose to the depot (1) and the observation to the central compartment (2).

3 The structural model

The model is a function with an ini block (starting values) and a model block (the structure). Fixed effects are estimated on the log scale, so the back-transformed CL, V, and Ka are always positive. Initial values are seeded from the Phase 2 NCA, which puts the optimiser close to the solution from the start.

print(base_model)
function () 
{
    ini({
        tka <- log(1.5)
        tcl <- log(2.8)
        tv <- log(32)
        eta.cl ~ 0.1
        add.sd <- 0.3
        prop.sd <- 0.1
    })
    model({
        ka <- exp(tka)
        cl <- exp(tcl + eta.cl)
        v <- exp(tv)
        d/dt(depot) <- -ka * depot
        d/dt(center) <- ka * depot - (cl/v) * center
        cp <- center/v
        cp ~ add(add.sd) + prop(prop.sd)
    })
}

The two d/dt(...) lines are the compartment mass-balance equations; cp = center/v is the predicted concentration; and cp ~ add(add.sd) + prop(prop.sd) specifies a combined additive + proportional residual error model. The base model carries between-subject variability on CL only (eta.cl) — variability on V and Ka is added in Phase 4.

4 Fitting the model (FOCEi)

fit_base_model() estimates the parameters by FOCEi (First-Order Conditional Estimation with interaction), the workhorse algorithm of population PK.

Code 
fit <- fit_base_model(dat)
calculating covariance matrix
done
Code 
fit

\[\begin{align*} {ka} & = \exp\left({tka}\right) \\ {cl} & = \exp\left({tcl}+{eta.cl}\right) \\ {v} & = \exp\left({tv}\right) \\ \frac{d \: depot}{dt} & = -{ka} {\times} {depot} \\ \frac{d \: center}{dt} & = {ka} {\times} {depot}-\left(\frac{{cl}}{{v}}\right) {\times} {center} \\ {cp} & = \frac{{center}}{{v}} \\ {cp} & \sim add({add.sd})+prop({prop.sd}) \end{align*}\]

5 Parameter estimates

Code 
base_param_table(fit) |>
  kbl(caption = "Base model fixed-effect estimates (back-transformed)",
      digits = 3) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
Base model fixed-effect estimates (back-transformed)
Parameter Est. SE %RSE Back-transformed(95%CI) BSV(CV%) Shrink(SD)%
tka 0.677 0.265 39.1 1.97 (1.17, 3.31)
tcl 1.04 0.0721 6.96 2.82 (2.45, 3.25) 23.5 7.91%<
tv 3.53 0.0454 1.29 34 (31.1, 37.2)
add.sd 0.225 0.225
prop.sd 0.259 0.259

5.1 Sanity check vs NCA

The population model and the model-free NCA should tell a consistent story:

Parameter NCA (geo. mean, Phase 2) Base model (typical)
CL (L/h) ~2.74 2.82
V (L) ~31.6 34
Ka (1/h) ~1.5 (from Tmax) 1.97

Close agreement is the key validation that the structural model and dataset are correct — the two independent methods converge on the same physiology.

6 Quick predicted-vs-observed check

Full diagnostics (GOF, VPC, CWRES) come in Phase 6; here we just confirm the fit is sensible — points should scatter around the line of identity.

Code 
fitdf <- as.data.frame(fit)

p <- ggplot2::ggplot(fitdf, ggplot2::aes(PRED, DV)) +
  ggplot2::geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  ggplot2::geom_point(alpha = 0.6, colour = "#2C3E50") +
  ggplot2::labs(x = "Population prediction (mg/L)", y = "Observed (mg/L)",
                title = "Observed vs population prediction (base model)")
p
Code 
save_fig(p, "03_base_obs_vs_pred")

7 Key takeaways

  • The data were re-expressed as a NONMEM event dataset (dose + observation rows).
  • A one-compartment oral model with first-order absorption was fitted by FOCEi.
  • Typical estimates (CL ≈ 2.82 L/h, V ≈ 34 L, Ka ≈ 1.97 /h) agree with the NCA — the structural model holds.
  • The base model includes IIV on CL only; the next phase adds variability on V and Ka.

Next: Phase 4 — IIV model, extending between-subject variability to V and Ka and examining the random-effect (η) distributions. ```
